title: The Serpent of Division
summary: The checked-in CME00027 XML is a University of Michigan Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse encoding of selected layers of Henry Noble MacCracken's 1911 edition of John Lydgate's Serpent of Division. The XML represents the edition title page, MacCracken's manuscript-and-print account on pp. 45-47, and the edited medieval work on pp. 49-67; it does not represent the whole 1911 volume. MacCracken supplied the lost opening of Fitzwilliam MS McClean 182 from British Library Add MS 48031 A (formerly Calthorpe/Yelverton 35), then transcribed McClean 182 for the rest, collating Magdalene Pepys MS 2006, Harvard Houghton MS Eng 530, the Treverys fragment, and occasionally Owen Rogers's 1559 print. A complete scan of the 1911 edition and a complete 432-canvas facsimile of Houghton MS Eng 530 are cached. The Fitzwilliam IIIF object exposes only two leaves outside the work; no complete public surrogate of Fitzwilliam MS McClean 182, British Library Add MS 48031 A, or Pepys MS 2006 was found on 2026-07-12, so exact catalog, onsite, and mediated reproduction routes are recorded.
review_notes: The intellectual work, four manuscript witnesses, early prints, MacCracken edition, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and modern facsimiles remain distinct lineage layers.

MacCracken's heading calls the Fitzwilliam source McLean MS 181, but his opening collation note, James's catalog, and the current Fitzwilliam record identify MS McClean 182; 181 is treated as an editorial error.
MacCracken's legacy Calthorpe foliation 146v-156v is not silently substituted for the current British Library locus ff. 165v-175v; both are preserved with their source context.
Complete-facsimile negatives are limited to public web access checked on 2026-07-12. They do not imply that repository photography, microfilm, or onsite consultation is impossible.
The Harvard IIIF bundle is complete relative to the exposed 432-canvas object and contains boards, endleaves, blanks, all leaves, and adjacent works, but no separate spine or edge views.

entities.label: General Corpus source XML for CME00027
entities.description: The XML artifact consumed by this repository's conversion and publication workflow, supplied through the pinned Kyvero-Press/CME git submodule.
notes: The file is 85,979 bytes at the recorded CME submodule commit.
Its TEMPHEAD records a review dated 2016-07-14; this does not establish the original keying or publication date.
The complete file contains 3 DIV1, 2 DIV2, 7 DIV3, 12 HEAD, 56 P, 4 LG, 28 L, 124 MILESTONE, 290 NOTE, and 24 PB elements.

entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text CME00027
entities.description: A public structured digital text derived selectively from MacCracken's 1911 edition, with edition-derived page milestones, headings, and notes.

date_statements.label: documented XML review
date_statements.notes: The TEMPHEAD date records review activity, not initial digitization.

notes: TEXT/@LANG is enm, appropriate to the medieval work; the encoded title and editorial apparatus are modern English.
The representation includes the 1911 title page, MacCracken's Manuscripts and Prints section on pp. 45-47, and the work text on pp. 49-67.
It omits the dedication, foreword, contents, introductory note on pp. 1-44, three full-page reproductions, notes on pp. 69-72, glossary on pp. 73-75, and colophon leaf.

entities.label: Henry Noble MacCracken's 1911 edition of The Serpent of Division
entities.description: A scholarly edition with historical and source introduction, an edited Middle English text based primarily on two manuscript witnesses, selected variants, notes, glossary, and three full-page manuscript reproductions.

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, The Serpent of Division, ed. Henry Noble MacCracken (London: Henry Frowde, Oxford University Press; New Haven: Yale University Press, 1911).
bibliographic.title: The Serpent of Division
bibliographic.publisher: Henry Frowde, Oxford University Press; Yale University Press
bibliographic.pages: 75 numbered pages with preliminary and final leaves, frontispiece, and two further plates

notes: MacCracken states that he prints a faithful transcription with modern punctuation, enclosing every alteration in brackets.
Calthorpe/Yelverton 35 supplies the opening missing from McClean 182 through printed p. 52; visual inspection resolves the OCR reading p. 53 to p. 52.
McClean 182 then supplies the base through the end; important variants from the other manuscripts and the Treverys print appear in footnotes, while Rogers 1559 is occasionally cited.

MacCracken's belief that the manuscripts are independent, that Fitzwilliam and Harvard may be closer, and that the Calthorpe ending may be an authorial early draft remains editorial conjecture rather than a settled stemma.

entities.label: Internet Archive scan of MacCracken's 1911 edition
entities.description: A complete public access scan of the bound printed volume from the University of Toronto's Robarts Library, digitized by Internet Archive in 2008.
entities.reproduction_format: 96-page color PDF derived from a 300 ppi capture; scan metadata maps 100 captured pages, including four calibration or color-card images excluded from access formats
notes: The access object contains both covers, end matter, all printed leaves, and the edition's three reproductions; it has no separately exposed spine or edge views.
The cached scan-data ZIP also contains capture profiles and calibration files and is recorded as metadata-only coverage.

entities.label: Fitzwilliam Museum MS McClean 182
entities.description: A fifteenth-century vellum codex whose opening work is Lydgate's Serpent of Division; the first leaf of the work is lost, and MacCracken used its surviving text as his principal base witness.

date_statements.label: production
date_statements.value: second quarter of the fifteenth century

holding.notes: The current object record was last updated 17 July 2025. MacCracken's section heading says McLean 181, but his own collation note and the current catalog identify 182.

physical_description.extent: 139 folios; The Serpent of Division occupies the first ten folios, with its first leaf lost
notes: The current record describes a green velvet binding.
MacCracken reports 35 lines per page and a clear clerical hand; these edition-derived details have not been checked against a complete current facsimile.
The opening leaf is absent; the surviving first ten folios contain the work, but a complete modern folio-by-folio target range was not established from the sparse current record.
James describes a complex continuation of the envoy across misbound leaves; complete-object images are needed before imposing a clean sequential locator.

entities.label: Fitzwilliam IIIF selection for MS McClean 182
entities.description: The current Fitzwilliam IIIF manifest exposes only two selected manuscript images, ff. 123v and 117r, both outside The Serpent of Division.
entities.reproduction_format: 2-canvas IIIF Presentation manifest; cached as two JPEG derivatives constrained to 1800 pixels
notes: This is a partial selection and cannot verify the work text, lost opening, envoy, binding, or full codex structure.

entities.label: British Library Add MS 48031 A
entities.description: A late-fifteenth-century paper manuscript containing a witness of The Serpent of Division at ff. 165v-175v, used by MacCracken for the opening missing from Fitzwilliam MS McClean 182 and for variant readings.

holding.notes: The current work locus is ff. 165v-175v. MacCracken's legacy foliation 146v-156v is preserved as historical evidence but not treated as current pagination.

physical_description.format: bound composite manuscript or precedent-book volume
notes: The current catalog describes a vellum binding with leather ties and manuscript fragments among the preliminary and final material.
A complete current surrogate is needed for a fuller whole-object physical description.

The current catalog identifies the work at ff. 165v-175v, dates its composition to 1422, and explains 1400 in the final colophon as a copyist's error because the text ends in the first year of Henry VI.
MacCracken describes this witness as a rapid, careless hand of about 1460 and conjectures that its divergent ending may preserve an early authorial draft; the latter claim remains unresolved.

entities.label: Magdalene College Pepys MS 2006
entities.description: A fifteenth-century miscellany containing The Serpent of Division on manuscript pages 191-209; MacCracken collated this text and reported inserted glosses, altered phrases, and occasional omissions.

holding.notes: The Pepys Library has no online manuscript catalog; James's 1923 printed catalog supplies the detailed object description.

physical_description.support: paper according to James's 1923 catalog; MacCracken called it vellum
physical_description.extent: 196 folios in James's catalog; MacCracken reported 391 pages
notes: The work occupies pp. 191-209 and its envoy ends on p. 209.
The conflicting support statements require direct object verification; James's full-codex description is preferred provisionally.
James records pasted-over material on adjacent pp. 190 and 210; those pages and all neighboring works belong in a complete-object request.

entities.label: Harvard Houghton Library MS Eng 530
entities.description: An English paper miscellany of about 1450 containing Lydgate's Damage and Destruction in Realms at ff. 49r-57v; the witness preserves an opening title but lacks the verse envoy.

holding.notes: The old IIIF endpoint redirects to the current 432-canvas Houghton manifest.

physical_description.format: manuscript codex in a tray case
notes: At least two unidentified hands in mixed cursive scripts, with flourished initials and rubrics.
Nineteenth-century blind-tooled calf binding.
Direct inspection locates the rubric and incipit at IIIF canvas 105 and Explicit at canvas 122.
The following canvas begins the adjacent Brut; no envoy appears between the Explicit and that next work.
MacCracken thought this witness possibly related to Fitzwilliam McClean 182 but did not establish a stemma.

entities.label: Harvard complete IIIF facsimile of Houghton MS Eng 530
entities.description: A complete current 432-canvas IIIF Presentation object exposing the bound manuscript from front board through rear board.
entities.reproduction_format: 432-canvas IIIF Presentation manifest; cached as JPEG derivatives constrained to 1800 pixels without upscaling
notes: The work occupies ff. 49r-57v, canvases 105-122; the bundle includes boards, endleaves, blanks, all manuscript leaves, and adjacent works, but no separate spine or edge views.

entities.label: Peter Treverys's fragmentary edition of Damage and Destruction in Realms
entities.description: An early black-letter octavo edition known to MacCracken through Joseph Haslewood's complete transcription of the surviving fragment; its lost title probably supplied The Serpent of Division to later print tradition.

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, Damage and Destruction in Realms, printed by Peter Treverys before 4 May 1559; fragment transcribed by Joseph Haslewood in Censura Literaria 9 (1809): 369 ff.
bibliographic.title: Damage and Destruction in Realms

notes: MacCracken states that Treverys used a good manuscript not identical with the four he described and may have modernized spelling.
The envoy follows the printer's colophon in this state but belongs to Lydgate's work, not to the printer.
No current holding or complete public surrogate of the fragment was identified during this review.

entities.label: Owen Rogers's 1559 edition of The Serpent of Division
entities.description: A black-letter octavo edition dated 4 May 1559, set forth after the author's old copy and judged by MacCracken to follow Treverys with possible reference to earlier manuscripts.

bibliographic.citation: John Lydgate, The Serpent of Division, set forth after the author's old copy (London: Owen Rogers, 4 May 1559).
bibliographic.title: The Serpent of Division

notes: The initials J. S. on the title page are retained as printed and not expanded to a named editorial agent without sufficient evidence.

entities.label: Internet Archive microfilm facsimile of the 1559 Rogers edition
entities.description: A public 63-image black-and-white microfilm-derived facsimile of a physical copy whose institution and copy identity are not exposed clearly enough in the provider metadata to equate it with MacCracken's copy.
entities.reproduction_format: 63-image black-and-white PDF derived from an 800 ppi microfilm capture; no binding exterior, spine, or edge views
notes: Complete is relative to the provider's exposed microfilm issue, not to a whole bound physical volume.

entities.label: M. R. James's 1912 description of McClean MS 182
entities.description: The printed Fitzwilliam collection catalog description of the complete McClean codex and its contents, including the opening Serpent witness and the complex leaf order around its envoy.

bibliographic.citation: M. R. James, A Descriptive Catalogue of the McClean Collection of Manuscripts in the Fitzwilliam Museum (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1912), pp. 349-352.
bibliographic.title: A Descriptive Catalogue of the McClean Collection of Manuscripts in the Fitzwilliam Museum

entities.label: M. R. James's 1923 description of Pepys MS 2006
entities.description: The printed Pepys Library medieval-manuscripts catalog description of the complete miscellany, its paper support, 196-folio extent, and Serpent locus at pp. 191-209.

bibliographic.citation: M. R. James, Bibliotheca Pepysiana: A Descriptive Catalogue of the Library of Samuel Pepys, Part III: Mediaeval Manuscripts (London: Sidgwick & Jackson, 1923), pp. 60-62.
bibliographic.title: Bibliotheca Pepysiana: Mediaeval Manuscripts

scope.notes: Repository provenance and matching identifiers support this digital-to-digital relation; it is not a claim of direct copying from a manuscript.

object.description: edition title page, Manuscripts and Prints pp. 45-47, and work text pp. 49-67

scope.notes: The encoding omits most preliminaries, the 44-page introduction, plates, notes, glossary, and colophon.

subject.description: edited work text from the point where McClean 182 begins on printed p. 52 through the end

object.description: surviving Serpent text in the codex's first ten folios after the lost opening leaf
locators: first ten folios; first leaf lost

scope.notes: MacCracken calls this his faithful base transcription, with modern punctuation and all alterations bracketed.

subject.description: opening printed text supplied before McClean 182 begins

object.description: opening portion within the Serpent witness

scope.notes: The exact switch point is visible on printed p. 52; the scan's OCR misreads the note as p. 53.

subject.description: edited medieval text and variant apparatus

scope.notes: The witness supplies the lost opening and remains a collated source for the rest, including its divergent ending.

subject.description: edited medieval text and important variant apparatus
prose tract and important variant apparatus

object.description: complete Harvard Serpent witness, which lacks the envoy

subject.description: edited medieval text and important variant apparatus

object.description: Treverys text as available through Haslewood's transcription of the fragment

subject.description: occasional readings in the edited text and apparatus

scope.notes: MacCracken had a complete transcript but says the 1559 print is only occasionally referred to in the printed collation.
MacCracken says Rogers certainly followed Treverys, while allowing possible reference to earlier manuscripts.

object.description: ff. 123v and 117r, both outside The Serpent of Division

scope.notes: The relation is complete only for the two selected leaves exposed by the IIIF manifest.

object.description: bound manuscript from front board through rear board

scope.notes: No separate spine or edge images are exposed.

object.description: the 1559 printed work in an unidentified physical copy

scope.notes: The physical copy is not assumed to be the copy used by MacCracken.

access.access_method: Request high-resolution photography or available microfilm through the Fitzwilliam Image Library, citing MS McClean 182 and object 239589.
access.format: repository-supplied photography or other available reproduction
notes: For a whole-object request specify covers or boards, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all 139 folios, and edges where offered; map the work's first ten folios separately and retain adjacent works.

access.access_method: Open the British Library Archives and Manuscripts record.
Request Add MS 48031 A for consultation in the British Library reading rooms, subject to current reader registration and handling rules.
The current catalog record was checked; its Digitised Content field exposes no images.
notes: This negative claim does not cover mediated reproduction or onsite access.

access.access_method: Request manuscript photography from British Library Digitisation Services using the exact shelfmark and folio range.
access.format: repository photography; manuscripts are photographed rather than scanned
notes: For whole-object preservation request all covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, manuscript fragments, all folios, ties, and edges where offered; separately identify work ff. 165v-175v. Reuse permission is distinct from image supply.

rights.component: University of Michigan electronic encoding and public digital text
rights.basis: The U-M item identifies the content as public domain and links the No Copyright - United States rights statement.
notes: This statement is scoped to U-M's exposed electronic item and does not license third-party manuscript images.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1911; U-M marks the digitized edition content public domain in the United States, and Internet Archive metadata records NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT after a 2008 copyright review.
notes: This does not by itself determine rights in modern photographs supplied by manuscript repositories or copyright status outside the United States.

rights.component: Internet Archive scan files and digital reproductions
rights.basis: The provider metadata records a NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT assessment for the scanned 1911 volume and exposes the derivative files publicly, but no explicit reusable-file license was found.
notes: The public-domain underlying volume and the scan derivative are modeled separately.

rights.component: Two Fitzwilliam manuscript photographs exposed by the IIIF manifest
rights.basis: The IIIF manifest attributes the images to the Fitzwilliam Museum and supplies a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 license.
notes: The cached files remain subject to the stated license; their presence in an ignored source cache does not authorize redistribution beyond it.

rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied by British Library Digitisation Services
rights.basis: British Library imaging terms distinguish image supply from reuse and may require separate permission, credit, and fees depending on use.
notes: Record the rights statement and order terms delivered with any future files before caching or publishing them.

rights.component: Any manuscript photographs supplied by the Pepys Library
rights.basis: Official guidance prohibits reader photography, offers a photographic service, states that Pepys Library digital images are copyright, and requires purchase or permission for publication images.
notes: Confirm exact terms for any commissioned files; this record does not assume that unpublished research use and publication use carry identical conditions.

rights.component: Harvard digital reproductions of Houghton MS Eng 530
rights.basis: Harvard's digitized-content terms state that it does not assert copyright over digital reproductions of works in the public domain in the United States; this fifteenth-century manuscript is public domain there. The item manifest itself carries attribution and viewer-terms links but no item-specific license.
notes: Users remain responsible for rights in other jurisdictions and for complying with Harvard's viewer terms and any privacy, publicity, cultural, or contractual restrictions.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1559, centuries before current United States copyright terms.
notes: This statement is separate from rights or contractual terms in the modern microfilm-derived facsimile.

rights.basis: The underlying 1559 edition is public domain and the provider exposes the files publicly, but its current metadata supplies no explicit rights field or reuse license for the microfilm derivative.
notes: Do not infer unrestricted reuse of the derivative merely from public download access.

editorial_practices.source_feature: The Fitzwilliam base witness lacks its first leaf.
editorial_practices.representation: Calthorpe/Yelverton 35 supplies the opening through printed p. 52; McClean 182 supplies the remainder.
editorial_practices.description: MacCracken constructs the printed base text from two manuscripts at an explicitly marked switch point rather than presenting a diplomatic transcription of one complete witness.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Manuscript punctuation in the two base witnesses
editorial_practices.description: MacCracken calls the edition a faithful transcription with modern punctuation and encloses every alteration in brackets.

editorial_practices.source_feature: Variant readings across four manuscripts and early prints
editorial_practices.representation: Important variants from the other manuscripts and Treverys in footnotes, with occasional reference to Rogers 1559
editorial_practices.description: The apparatus is selective; MacCracken did not print his full comparative study or a complete variant inventory.

editorial_practices.source_feature: A 1911 volume containing preliminaries, introduction, manuscript images, edited text, notes, glossary, and colophon
editorial_practices.representation: Title-page content, manuscript-and-print account pp. 45-47, and edited work pp. 49-67
editorial_practices.description: U-M selected the work and a small amount of edition context rather than encoding the complete physical or intellectual contents of the 1911 volume.

evidence.summary: Technical evidence for the 63-image microfilm-derived issue, page sequence, and exposed access coverage.

evidence.citation: Direct visual inspection of the cached Internet Archive microfilm PDF of Owen Rogers's 1559 edition.
evidence.locator: title page, opening text, work end, and printer colophon across the 63-image issue
evidence.summary: Confirms The Serpent of Division title, Lydgate attribution, J. S. initials, 4 May 1559 date, Owen Rogers imprint, complete provider-exposed text, and absence of binding exterior views.

evidence.citation: Maura Nolan, 'Tragic history: Lydgate's Serpent of Division,' in John Lydgate and the Making of Public Culture (Cambridge University Press, 2005), doi:10.1017/CBO9780511483387.002.
evidence.summary: Modern scholarly evidence framing the work as a short prose tract written in 1422, recounting Caesar and warning lords and princes about political and social division after Henry V's death.

open_questions.question: Can a complete current color facsimile of Fitzwilliam MS McClean 182 be obtained to verify MacCracken's principal base witness, lost opening, envoy sequence, and codex boundaries directly?
next_steps: Request a whole-object reproduction through the Fitzwilliam Image Library.
Specify boards, binding, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, all 139 folios, edges where offered, and the work's first ten folios separately.
Record supplied-file rights and reconcile James's complex leaf sequence before caching.

open_questions.question: Can a complete color reproduction of British Library Add MS 48031 A be obtained to verify the Calthorpe/Yelverton opening, divergent ending, legacy-to-current foliation, and whole-object structure?
next_steps: Request complete-object photography through British Library Digitisation Services.
Include all covers, spine, fragments, ties, endleaves, blanks, folios, and edges where offered; map current ff. 165v-175v separately.
Record order and reuse terms before caching and collate the work against MacCracken.

open_questions.question: Can Pepys MS 2006 be photographed completely to verify work pp. 191-209, the envoy, pasted-over adjacent pages, and the conflict between James's paper support and MacCracken's vellum description?
next_steps: Contact the Pepys Librarian for a research appointment and whole-object photography quote.
Request covers, spine, endleaves, blanks, all 196 folios or 391 pages, edges where offered, and pp. 190-210 as an explicitly mapped work neighborhood.
Record supplied-file rights and reconcile the physical-description conflict from direct images or repository advice.

open_questions.question: What is the current location, physical identity, exact date, and survival status of the Treverys fragment used through Haslewood by MacCracken?
next_steps: Trace Haslewood's source through Censura Literaria and later bibliographies.
Search current ESTC/STC copy records, auction provenance, and institutional catalogs for the fragment or its photographs.
Keep the entity's survival status unknown and do not infer that the fragment is lost.

open_questions.question: Which physical copy underlies the Internet Archive microfilm of Owen Rogers's 1559 edition?
next_steps: Resolve microfilm identifier IA40313002-85 through the provider or source-vendor catalog.
Compare defects and annotations with identified surviving copies of the 1559 edition.
Do not identify this surrogate with MacCracken's complete transcript source until copy-level evidence supports it.
